items.title: The Supply Chain Behind One of the U.S.’ Largest Food Dye Companies
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>RFK Jr. says it is working with the food industry to remove synthetic food dyes from the food supply by the end of 2026. But dye makers say meeting this deadline safely could take more than six years. WSJ visited Sensient Technologies, one of the largest natural food color plants in the world, to understand why the 2026 deadline is so hard to achieve and what it takes to create natural food dyes.

#RFKJr #Food #Shorts</article>

items.title: Engineer Explains Three Key Issues in Renewable Grid Design
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Renewable energy has created a hidden infrastructure challenge. While solar and wind power now make up a larger share of the electricity supply, the power grid wasn’t designed to handle them. WSJ asked a power grid engineer to break down the mechanics of renewable energy to explain three ways to make the grid more resilient to disruptions.

#Energy #Infrastructure #Shorts</article>

items.title: These Gen Z Men Voted for Trump. Would They Do It Again? | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Young men helped carry President Trump to office. Half a year into his term, Trump has passed a massive GOP tax megabill, impacted global trade with tariffs, bombed Iran’s nuclear facilities and more.  

WSJ asked six of these young voters about how they think the president is living up to his campaign promises on issues like immigration and mass deportations, whether or not the U.S. needed to strike Iran, if DOGE and Elon Musk did a successful job and more. 

Chapters:
0:00 Trump’s early promises
0:33 Is Trump succeeding with mass deportations?
1:38 Should there be an easier process for immigration?
2:57 Is Trump doing a good job with foreign policy?
3:37 Did the U.S. need to strike Iran?
5:17 Should the U.S. do more to help Palestinians?
6:20 Should the U.S. be more involved in the Russia-Ukraine War?
7:06 Is Trump being transparent with the Epstein files?
7:36 Are you optimistic about the economy?
8:35 Is Trump helping you?
9:22 Did Elon Musk and DOGE cut waste?
11:00 Would you change your vote?

#Trump #Politics #WSJ</article>

items.title: Inside China’s Dark Factories, Where Hyper-Automation Rules 24/7
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Ford’s CEO says China’s EV industry poses an existential threat to global automakers. The country’s dark factories, which have robots run 24/7 with limited human interaction,  aim to further widen the gap between Chinese car manufacturers and everyone else. As China  grapples with overproduction and Trump’s trade war with Beijing continues, one big question remains: who will buy all these vehicles? WSJ visited Zeekr, a Chinese luxury EV-maker, to learn about China’s hyper-automation.

#China #Factory #Shorts</article>

items.title: Tech Investor Breaks Down AI Trading Trends
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Imran Khan, founder and chief investment officer of Proem Asset Management, joins Gunjan to talk about the AI trade surrounding companies like Nvidia, Meta and Alphabet. Plus, Khan chats about how AI enthusiasm is driving market speculation, herd thinking in markets, and the value of private-market investments like OpenAI. 

#AI #Investing #Shorts</article>

items.title: The U.S. Is at WWII Levels of Debt. Will It Sink the Economy?
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>One of the core issues between Elon Musk and President Donald Trump’s feud is over Republican’s “big, beautiful bill” in Congress. Musk is concerned about how much it raises the national debt. 

The U.S. national debt is on its way to $30 trillion dollars and is projected to be more than 100% of GDP at the end of this year. So is that… bad? Let’s look at what the debt is, how it affects the economy and how much is too much?

#Debt #Economy #Shorts</article>

items.title: The Money-Making Secrets Behind Hotel Design | WSJ Pro Perfected
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>From vanishing minibars to disappearing closets, hotel rooms are shrinking. With the rise of Airbnb and hotel occupancy rates plateauing, operators are on the hunt for the most profitable design to maximize profits. Take Marriott’s Moxy brand, for example — its rooms are less than half the average size yet can generate up to 20% more revenue than its peers.

WSJ speaks to a leading hotel designer to find out how some hotels are keeping guests happy with less space.

Chapters:
0:00 Why your hotel closet vanished
0:39 How to shrink a hotel room
3:04 Encouraging guests down to the bar
5:02 Finding opportunities in the local area

Pro Perfected
Experts in engineering and design break down a ubiquitous problem, examining how the world is built and what can make it better.

#Hotels #Design #WSJ</article>

items.title: Are Private Markets Like OpenAI and SpaceX Overvalued?

<article>Imran Khan, founder and chief investment officer of Proem Asset Management, joins WSJ to talk about the AI trade surrounding companies like Nvidia, Meta and Alphabet. Plus, Khan chats about how AI enthusiasm is driving market speculation, herd thinking in markets, and the value of private-market investments like OpenAI. 

#Investing #AI #Shorts</article>

items.title: This Trump Poll Reveals a Number That Should ‘Scare’ Democrats | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>WSJ’s latest poll shows President Trump’s approval rating holding steady. But where do voters land on Trump’s tariffs, the “big, beautiful bill,” immigration and more?

WSJ’s Aaron Zitner breaks down the most recent Wall Street Journal poll, conducted between July 16-20.

Chapters:
0:00 WSJ’s latest poll
0:37 Trump actions in second term
1:28 Economic news
2:14 Tarnished brand of Democrats
2:47 Democrats and healthcare
3:46 2026 midterms

#Trump #Politics #WSJ</article>

#Harvard #Trump #Shorts</article>

items.title: Why This Bay Is Key to Russia’s Militarization of the Arctic
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>The Kola Bay is emerging as one of Russia’s most powerful hubs in its quest to dominate the Arctic. This small stretch of water is where Moscow stores part of its icebreaker fleet and could mine for minerals and oil, export those resources and launch nuclear weapons. WSJ explores why Kola Bay is so critical for Russia’s fight for the Arctic. 

#Russia #Arctic #Shorts</article>
